content.problemStatement:
In an undirected unweighted graph, an edge of the graph is said to be lucky if it is a part of some cycle of the graph.

You are given a list **E** of **M** edges. We define **f(i)** as the number of intervals **[l, r]** (**1 ≤ l ≤ i ≤ r ≤ M**) such that if you build a graph from edges **El, El+1, ..., Er**, the edge **Ei** will be a lucky edge in this graph.

Your task is to calculate the values of **f(1), f(2), ..., f(M)**.
constraints: 1 ≤ sum of M over all test-cases ≤ 20000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing an integer M, the number of edges. Each of the next M lines contains two space-separated integers ui and vi, denoting the i-th edge connects nodes ui and vi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing M integers, where the i-th integer is the value of f(i), representing the number of intervals [l, r] (1 ≤ l ≤ i ≤ r ≤ M) such that if you build a graph from edges El, El+1, ..., Er, the edge Ei will be a lucky edge in this graph.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the intervals are [1,1], [1,2], [1,3], [2,2], [2,3], [3,3]. For the edge (1,2), it is part of a cycle in the intervals [1,3] and [2,3], so f(1) = 1. For the edge (3,4), it is not part of any cycle in any interval, so f(2) = 0. For the edge (2,1), it is part of a cycle in the interval [1,3], so f(3) = 1.
For this test case, all edges are part of a cycle in all intervals they are part of, so f(i) = 3 for all i.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: Expected output for the maximum boundary case

hidden.label: general case 1
hidden.expectedOutput: Expected output for the general case 1

hidden.label: general case 2
hidden.expectedOutput: Expected output for the general case 2

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a Union-Find data structure to keep track of the connected components in the graph. We iterate over each edge and check if adding it to the graph creates a cycle. If it does, we increment the count of lucky edges for the current interval. We use a nested loop to consider all possible intervals [l, r] for each edge i.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M^3) — because for each test case, we have a nested loop structure that checks all intervals for each edge, and within that, we perform Union-Find operations which take nearly constant time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M + N) — where M is the number of edges and N is the number of nodes, because we need to store the parent and rank information for each node in the Union-Find data structure, and also store the edges.
